title: The Dative Case (Dativ)
description: The dative case marks the indirect object — the person or thing that receives the benefit or result of an action. It is also required after certain prepositions and with specific verbs that inherently take a dative object.
whenToUse: For the indirect object (the receiver or beneficiary of an action)
After the prepositions aus, bei, mit, nach, seit, von, zu, gegenüber
With verbs that inherently take the dative: helfen, gefallen, gehören, danken, folgen, gratulieren, etc.
After two-way prepositions (Wechselpräpositionen) when indicating location (no movement toward)

rules.title: The indirect object takes the dative
rules.explanation: The indirect object is the person who receives something or benefits from the action. Ask "Wem?" (to whom?) to identify it. In sentences with both a direct and indirect object, the indirect object is in the dative.

examples.correct: Ich gebe dem Mann das Buch.
examples.incorrect: Ich gebe den Mann das Buch.
examples.explanation: "Dem Mann" is the indirect object (to whom?). Masculine "der" becomes "dem" in the dative.

examples.correct: Sie schenkt der Freundin eine Blume.
examples.explanation: "Der Freundin" is the indirect object. Feminine "die" becomes "der" in the dative.

examples.correct: Er zeigt dem Kind das Bild.
examples.explanation: "Dem Kind" is the indirect object. Neuter "das" becomes "dem" in the dative.

rules.title: Dative prepositions: aus, bei, mit, nach, seit, von, zu
rules.explanation: These prepositions always require the dative case. A common mnemonic is "AUS-BEI-MIT nach SEIT-VON-ZU" (sung to the tune of "Blue Danube"). Also: gegenüber always takes the dative.

examples.explanation: After "aus", the neuter article changes to "dem".

examples.correct: Sie fährt mit dem Bus zur Arbeit.
examples.explanation: After "mit", the masculine article becomes "dem". Note: "zur" = "zu der" (dative contraction).

examples.correct: Seit einem Jahr wohne ich in Berlin.

rules.title: Verbs that take the dative
rules.explanation: Some German verbs always require a dative object instead of an accusative one. These must be memorized. Common ones: helfen (to help), gefallen (to please), gehören (to belong to), danken (to thank), folgen (to follow), antworten (to answer), gratulieren (to congratulate).

examples.explanation: "Helfen" takes the dative. Use "dem Schüler", not "den Schüler".
"Gefallen" takes a dative object: "der Lehrerin" (fem. dative).
"Gehören" takes the dative: "meinem Vater" (masc. dative of "mein").

rules.title: Dative article changes for all genders
rules.explanation: In the dative, ALL genders change their articles — not just masculine. Masculine: der → dem, feminine: die → der, neuter: das → dem, plural: die → den (and the noun adds -n if possible).

examples.explanation: Plural: die → den, and the noun adds "-n" (Kind → Kindern).

rules.title: Plural nouns add -n in the dative
rules.explanation: In the dative plural, the article changes to "den" and the noun itself adds an -n ending — unless the plural already ends in -n or -s.

examples.explanation: Plural "die Schüler" becomes "den Schülern" — article changes and -n is added.
"Die Frauen" already ends in -n, so only the article changes to "den".

commonMistakes.explanation: "Helfen" requires the dative, not the accusative. Neuter "das" must become "dem".

commonMistakes.correct: Sie geht zu der Schule. / Sie geht zur Schule.

commonMistakes.explanation: After "mit" (dative), plural "die" becomes "den" and the noun adds "-n".
"Gefallen" takes the dative. Feminine possessive "meine" becomes "meiner".
"Bei" requires the dative. Masculine "mein" becomes "meinem", not "meinen" (which is accusative).

commonMistakes.correct: Er dankt den Lehrern für die Hilfe.
commonMistakes.incorrect: Er dankt die Lehrer für die Hilfe.
commonMistakes.explanation: "Danken" takes the dative. Plural "die Lehrer" becomes "den Lehrern".

tips: Ask "Wem?" (to whom / for whom?) to identify the dative object in a sentence.
Memorize the dative prepositions with the mnemonic melody: "AUS-BEI-MIT nach SEIT-VON-ZU fährst du immer mit der U" (a well-known German learner rhyme).
Unlike the accusative, the dative changes articles for ALL genders — not just masculine. Pay special attention to feminine (die → der), which looks like the masculine nominative.
Learn dative verbs as a group: helfen, gefallen, gehören, danken, folgen, antworten, gratulieren, glauben, passen, schmecken.
Do not forget the -n on plural nouns in the dative (den Kindern, den Häusern) unless the plural already ends in -n or -s.
